extra.reasoning:
Property: NOT(rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b).

rec_drug_a is set true by exactly one rule, rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50), whose condition is the full six-way conjunction C = (heart_failure ∧ anticoagulated ∧ seizure_history ∧ immunosuppressed ∧ polypharmacy ∧ diabetes). No other rule touches rec_drug_a, and the default is false. Therefore rec_drug_a = true if and only if C holds.

rec_drug_b is governed by two rules: guard_suppress_b (priority 90, condition C → rec_drug_b := false) and rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50, condition heart_failure → rec_drug_b := true), with default false.

Consider the only case where rec_drug_a can be true, i.e., C true. C includes heart_failure as a conjunct, so heart_failure is necessarily true, meaning rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50) fires to push rec_drug_b := true. But guard_suppress_b also fires (its condition is C, which is true) at priority 90 > 50, and per-output highest-priority-wins resolution selects it, forcing rec_drug_b := false. Hence whenever rec_drug_a = true, rec_drug_b = false.

In every other case (C false), rec_drug_a = false (default), so the conjunction rec_drug_a ∧ rec_drug_b is trivially false regardless of rec_drug_b.

Thus rec_drug_a = true ⟹ rec_drug_b = false across the entire 2^7 input space; the two outputs are never jointly true. The higher-priority guard rule (90) is exactly what prevents the otherwise-conflicting priority-50 b-trigger from co-firing with the a-recommendation. The mutual-exclusion property holds.
